skill.name: baoyu-compress-image
skill.description: Compress image files from the command line using WebP, PNG, or JPEG output. The skill chooses sips, cwebp, ImageMagick, or Sharp based on the local system.
skill.summary: Compress images locally with configurable format, quality, directory, and JSON output options.
skill.author: jimliu

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command-execution patterns, but the script uses argument arrays for expected image tools and I found no shell string execution or network exfiltration. The real risks are local external image parsers, default file replacement, dynamic Sharp loading, and documented EXTEND.md overrides from project or home paths.

medium_findings.title: External Image Tools Process User Files
medium_findings.description: The script invokes sips, cwebp, ImageMagick convert, and Sharp against user-selected image paths. Arguments are passed as arrays, which reduces shell injection risk, but external image parsers still process untrusted files locally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is explicit and semantically part of compression. I found no shell interpolation, so the risk is tool exposure to untrusted media rather than direct command injection.

medium_findings.title: Original Files Can Be Replaced By Default
medium_findings.description: When keep mode is not enabled and output differs from input, the script deletes the original image and renames the temporary output. This behavior is documented, but it can cause data loss if users expect non-destructive compression.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file deletion and rename logic is direct and documented in the command examples. This is a product safety risk, not evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Local Extension Files Can Override Skill Defaults
medium_findings.description: The documentation tells agents to load project and home EXTEND.md files before workflow and says extension content overrides defaults. A project-controlled extension file could alter agent behavior if trusted without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The override behavior is plainly documented, but no malicious EXTEND.md file is present in the scanned files. The concern is a prompt-injection surface created by future local files.

low_findings.title: Command Documentation Triggered Static Shell Warnings
low_findings.description: Many SKILL.md findings are examples showing how to run the compression script. They are user-facing command snippets, not hidden execution logic inside the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced examples and descriptive output. They do not add execution paths beyond the documented CLI behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match words such as MD in Markdown file names or documentation. I found no hashing, encryption, or cryptographic algorithm use in the reviewed code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptions, table text, or image extension handling, not cryptographic operations. No evidence of weak crypto use was found.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Benign Error Messages
low_findings.description: The flagged invalid format and invalid quality lines print user input validation errors. I found no collection of host information or transmission of system data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are ordinary CLI validation branches. They do not query system properties or send data outside the process.

dangerous_patterns.title: child_process.spawn Used For Local Tools
dangerous_patterns.description: The script imports child_process and spawns which, sips, cwebp, and convert. The implementation avoids shell strings, but marketplace users should expect local command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The spawn usage is explicit and centralized. The risk is well bounded because command arguments are arrays and tool names come from fixed code paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Import Of Sharp
dangerous_patterns.description: The Sharp library is loaded dynamically when system compressors are not used. This is a normal fallback pattern, but it can introduce dependency and native image parser risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic import is visible and tied to the image compression fallback. I found no obfuscated module name or remote import URL.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Mutation During Compression
dangerous_patterns.description: The script reads directories, writes temporary outputs, deletes originals, and renames compressed files. These operations are expected for the skill but require clear user consent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are direct and required for output handling. They do not target hidden paths or unrelated files in the implementation.

content.user_title: Compress Images for Web and Apps
content.value_statement: Large image files slow websites, apps, and documentation workflows. This skill compresses local images with configurable formats, quality, and directory processing.
actual_capabilities: Compresses single image files from a local path.
Processes directories and can recurse through nested folders.
Outputs WebP by default and supports PNG and JPEG targets.
Lets users set output path, quality, and keep-original behavior.
Uses available local compressors before falling back to Sharp.
Can return plain text or JSON-formatted compression results.
limitations: It requires Bun through the documented npx command.
Compression quality depends on installed local image tools.
Original files may be replaced unless keep mode is used.
It does not upload images or provide cloud compression.

use_cases.title: Prepare Web Assets
use_cases.description: Compress product images, screenshots, and documentation media before publishing a website.

use_cases.title: Reduce Repository Media Size
use_cases.description: Batch-compress images in a project folder to reduce repository size and review payloads.

use_cases.title: Optimize Support Screenshots
use_cases.description: Convert large screenshots into smaller files before sharing them in tickets or docs.

prompt_templates.title: Compress One Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Compress this image to WebP at the default quality: ./assets/photo.png

prompt_templates.title: Keep The Original
prompt_templates.prompt: Compress ./screenshots/home.png to PNG quality 75 and keep the original file.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize A Folder
prompt_templates.scenario: Batch compression for a project directory
prompt_templates.prompt: Compress all supported images in ./public/images recursively at quality 70 and show a summary.

prompt_templates.title: Create Scriptable Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Compress ./media/banner.jpg to WebP quality 65 and return JSON output for a build script.

output_examples.input: Compress a product image to WebP at quality 80.
output_examples.output: The skill reports the original path, new WebP path, before-and-after file sizes, reduction percentage, and compressor used.
The skill lists each processed image and ends with total files processed, total size reduction, and overall compression ratio.

output_examples.input: Compress an image with JSON output.
output_examples.output: The skill returns structured fields for input, output, input size, output size, ratio, and selected compressor.

best_practices: Use keep mode when compressing original source images.
Test quality settings on a few images before batch processing.
Review local EXTEND.md files before letting them change workflow behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not run recursive compression on folders without backups.
Do not process untrusted image files on sensitive systems.
Do not assume every system has the same compressor installed.

faq.question: What output format does this skill use by default?
faq.answer: It outputs WebP by default unless the user selects PNG or JPEG.

faq.question: Can it process more than one image?
faq.answer: Yes. It can process a directory and can recurse through nested folders.

faq.question: Does it keep the original image?
faq.answer: Only when keep mode is enabled. Otherwise, default conversion can replace the original.

faq.question: Which compression tools can it use?
faq.answer: It can use sips, cwebp, ImageMagick convert, or Sharp depending on the platform and requested format.

faq.question: Does this skill send images to a remote service?
faq.answer: No evidence of upload or network transfer was found in the reviewed files.

faq.question: What security warning should users know?
faq.answer: The skill runs local image tools and may load local EXTEND.md instructions, so review inputs and extensions first.
